options.feedback_wrong: Anticholinergic toxidrome can cause fatal arrhythmias, hyperthermia-induced seizures, and rhabdomyolysis. It requires active monitoring and management. 'Self-limiting' is only true with proper supportive care — it is not benign.

options.text: Torsades de Pointes — QT prolongation monitoring and magnesium ready
options.feedback_correct: Correct. QT 480ms with tachycardia creates significant risk of Torsades de Pointes — a polymorphic ventricular tachycardia that can degenerate into VF. Continuous cardiac monitoring is mandatory. IV magnesium sulfate should be available. Avoid any additional QT-prolonging agents.

options.feedback_wrong: Hyperkalemia from rhabdomyolysis is not the primary concern in anticholinergic toxidrome. The immediate threat is QT prolongation and Torsades de Pointes from anticholinergic cardiotoxicity.

Pulmonary edema is not the primary concern here. The main arrhythmia risk from anticholinergic QT prolongation is Torsades de Pointes. This patient needs cardiac monitoring, not respiratory precautions specifically.

management.title: Disposition
management.hint: HR 128, QT 480ms, temp 38.6, urinary retention, agitation. Where does she go?

options.feedback_wrong: Physostigmine is an IV medication used in acute toxidrome management — it is not dispensed as an outpatient oral prescription. This patient requires inpatient monitoring and IV management.

options.text: Discharge after 2 hours of observation if vitals improve
options.feedback_wrong: Two hours is not adequate. Belladonna alkaloids have a prolonged duration of action — effects can last 24-48 hours. QT prolongation and hyperthermia require sustained monitoring. She needs admission.

options.text: Transfer to psychiatry for agitation management
options.feedback_wrong: Her agitation is organic — caused by anticholinergic delirium. This is a medical emergency, not a psychiatric presentation. Psychiatric transfer would be inappropriate and dangerous without medical stabilization.

options.text: Admit to monitored bed — toxicology, continuous telemetry, physostigmine if needed
options.feedback_correct: Correct. Admission with continuous telemetry is mandatory given QT prolongation and tachycardia. Toxicology consult. Serial vital signs and neuro assessments. She will likely improve over 12-24 hours as the alkaloids are metabolized, but the arrhythmia window requires monitoring.

report_template.presenting: acute anticholinergic toxidrome following two hours of unprotected dermal exposure to Atropa belladonna during herbalist work

pertinent_positives.addl-hx: two hours bare-handed grinding of belladonna berries and leaves, facial contact, symptom onset within two hours — blurred vision first, then dry mouth, then palpitations, now urinary retention
pertinent_positives.pe: fixed maximally dilated pupils 8mm bilaterally, flushed hot dry skin with complete anhidrosis, HR 128, temp 38.6, distended bladder with 680mL retention on ultrasound, absent bowel sounds
pertinent_positives.labs: mildly elevated glucose, QT prolongation to 480ms on EKG — arrhythmia risk
pertinent_positives.imaging: CT head without acute intracranial process, bladder scan confirming urinary retention
pertinent_positives.special: serum and urine toxicology positive for atropine and tropane alkaloids confirming belladonna anticholinergic toxidrome

report_template.assessment: Anticholinergic Toxidrome — Atropa belladonna dermal and mucosal exposure, complicated by urinary retention, ileus, hyperthermia, and QT prolongation
report_template.plan: IV benzodiazepines for agitation, urinary catheterization, active cooling, continuous telemetry for QT monitoring, IV magnesium on standby, physostigmine if refractory agitation or hemodynamic compromise, toxicology consult, admit for 24-48hr monitoring

explanation: Anticholinergic toxidrome: dry as a bone (anhidrosis), blind as a bat (mydriasis), red as a beet (flushing), hot as a hare (hyperthermia), mad as a hatter (delirium). Caused by muscarinic blockade — atropine, scopolamine, belladonna, jimsonweed, diphenhydramine. Antidote: physostigmine (crosses BBB). Never give atropine. Never give haloperidol. Distinguish from sympathomimetic (also hot/tachy/agitated but DIAPHORETIC — key difference).

title: The Seamstress's Sorrow

chart.cc: I don't know why I'm here, really. I'm just tired. All the time. And sad, I suppose. My supervisor sent me. She says I've been crying at my bench.
chart.hpi: The Seamstress arrived alone, her coat buttoned to the collar despite the mild evening. She moves carefully, as if conserving something. She has been the lead embroiderer at the Threadneedle Textile Works for six years — fine detail work under lamplight, twelve-hour shifts. Over the past four months she has felt a creeping, total exhaustion that she cannot sleep off. She weeps without knowing why. She has lost interest in her embroidery patterns, which once consumed her. Her supervisor noticed she had been making errors — dropped stitches, misaligned seams — and sent her here with a note that reads simply: 'Something is wrong with her.' She was treated for depression at a guild physician two months ago. The sertraline has done nothing. Her menstrual cycles have always been heavy — she mentions this only when directly asked, as if it is too ordinary a detail to bother with.
chart.pmh: Depression — diagnosed two months ago at guild physician. Dysmenorrhea.
chart.meds: Sertraline 50mg daily (started 8 weeks ago). Iron-poor diet — skips meals during busy production seasons.
chart.fh: Mother: anaemia in her thirties, details unknown. Father: well.
chart.sh: Master seamstress, Threadneedle Textile Works, Cogsworth District. Lives alone in the workers' quarter above the mill. Twelve-hour shifts, six days a week. Non-smoker. Does not drink. Has not been eating properly — bread and tea most days during the winter rush.

cc_after_addl_hx: The periods have always been heavy. Seven, eight days. I just thought — I thought that was normal. Nobody ever told me it wasn't.
cc_after_pe: My nails have been like this for months. I kept filing them down. I didn't know they were trying to tell me something.
cc_after_imaging: My heart is alright though? At least tell me my heart is alright.
cc_after_hint: Please. I just want to know what's wrong with me. I'm so tired of not knowing.
cc_after_dx: Iron. That's all it is? All these months of crying at my bench — and it was iron?
fennick_after_addl_hx: "Seven to eight days. Clots. And not one person in her life thought to ask whether that was normal. Not one."
fennick_after_pe: "Look at her nail beds. Look at her conjunctivae. The colour of old linen. This is not a woman who is depressed, Eddie. This is a woman who is running dry."
fennick_after_imaging: "The chest is clear. The heart is holding. But a machine that runs without fuel does not stay intact forever. Look elsewhere."
additional_hx: She answers questions with the quiet precision of someone accustomed to following instructions, but her eyes are glassy with a tiredness that feels older than four months. When you ask about her periods, she pauses — then describes them as 'rather heavy, always have been.' Seven to eight days. Soaking through pads. Clots. She has never once sought help for it because no one told her she should. She craves ice — has been chewing it constantly from the ice cart outside the mill, which she finds embarrassing to admit. She has not been eating real meals since November. When you ask if she ever feels short of breath on the stairs, something shifts in her expression — recognition. Yes. Every morning. She thought she was just unfit. The sertraline hasn't touched the sadness. Nothing has. She looks at her hands when she says this — they are the hands of a woman who has spent her life making beautiful things, and right now her nails are brittle and she cannot hold a needle steady.
patient_quote_addl_hx: "I keep thinking I should be able to push through it. I've always pushed through things. But it's like pushing through water. And the crying — I don't even know what I'm crying about. I just cry. The ice helps somehow. I know that sounds strange."
pe_findings: General: Pale, thin, fatigued-appearing woman. Quiet affect, appropriate. Skin: marked pallor of face, palms, and nail beds. Koilonychia — bilateral spoon-shaped nails, most pronounced on index and middle fingers. Angular cheilitis at bilateral mouth corners. Hair: diffuse thinning. Eyes: conjunctival pallor bilaterally — the palpebral conjunctivae are the colour of old linen. Tongue: mild glossitis, smooth and tender. Nails: brittle, longitudinally ridged. CV: tachycardic rate 108, regular. Soft systolic flow murmur at left sternal border — high-output state. No JVP elevation, no oedema. Resp: clear. Abdomen: soft, non-tender, no organomegaly. No lymphadenopathy.
imaging: CXR: Normal cardiac silhouette. No infiltrate, no effusion. Mild cardiomegaly not present — the flow murmur is functional. No bony abnormalities. No hilar lymphadenopathy. Not the diagnostic study of choice here — blood work is what you need.

special_test.name: Peripheral Blood Smear
special_test.result: Hypochromic microcytic red blood cells — pale centres larger than normal, pencil cells, occasional target cells. Marked anisocytosis and poikilocytosis. RDW elevated. Platelets mildly elevated (reactive thrombocytosis — classic in iron deficiency). No spherocytes, no schistocytes, no hypersegmented neutrophils. Morphology is unambiguously consistent with iron deficiency anaemia.

memory_fragments.cbc: The roses she embroidered once blessed strangers' weddings with red. She cannot remember now what red feels like. As if the red inside her has simply... run dry.
memory_fragments.pe_koilonychia: Her nails built a thousand things. Now they are spoons — hollow, curved inward, as if her own hands are begging to be filled with something she no longer has.
memory_fragments.addl_hx_ice: Chewing ice is the only moment of clarity she gets. In the heat of the mill, surrounded by steam and noise, it is the last cold thing keeping her from dissolving entirely.

tests.name: CBC
tests.result: Hgb 7.4 g/dL (CRITICALLY LOW). MCV 64 fL (LOW — microcytic). MCH 19 pg (LOW). MCHC 28 g/dL (LOW — hypochromic). RDW 18.2% (HIGH). WBC 7.1 (normal). Platelets 487 (mildly elevated — reactive thrombocytosis, classic in iron deficiency).
tests.memory_fragment: The roses she embroidered once blessed strangers' weddings with red. She cannot remember now what red feels like. As if the red inside her has simply... run dry.

tests.name: CMP
tests.result: Normal. Creatinine 0.7, glucose 82, LFTs normal, albumin 3.4 (mildly low — poor nutritional intake). No metabolic derangement.

tests.name: EKG
tests.result: Sinus tachycardia rate 108. No ischemic changes. Tachycardia is a compensatory response to severe anaemia — high-output state. No arrhythmia.

tests.name: Troponin
tests.result: Negative. No myocardial injury. The flow murmur is not from structural cardiac disease.

tests.name: Lipase
tests.result: Normal. Not relevant to this presentation.

tests.name: UA
tests.result: Normal. No haematuria. No protein. No infection. No haemoglobinuria to suggest haemolysis.

tests.name: ABG/VBG
tests.result: pH 7.42, pO2 96 on room air. Normal. Her oxygen saturation is fine — the problem is not oxygenation, it is oxygen-carrying capacity. SpO2 is misleading in severe anaemia.

tests.name: Coags
tests.result: Normal. PT/INR 1.0, PTT 28. No coagulopathy. Heavy menstrual bleeding is not from a clotting disorder in this case.

hints: The sertraline has done nothing in eight weeks. That is not a treatment failure — that is a diagnostic clue. Her sadness is not primary. It is downstream of something physical. What does profound anaemia do to a person's mood, energy, and cognition?
Tachycardia at rest, flow murmur, conjunctival pallor, koilonychia, angular cheilitis, glossitis, craving ice. She is not depressed. She is exsanguinating slowly. The CBC will tell you everything.
Microcytic hypochromic anaemia in a menstruating woman who skips meals and has heavy periods for seven days every cycle. The iron stores are empty. Check ferritin, serum iron, TIBC.
Pica — the craving for ice specifically — is called pagophagia. It is one of the most specific signs of iron deficiency anaemia. When a patient tells you they cannot stop eating ice, believe them.

management.title: Immediate investigation to confirm
management.hint: The CBC strongly suggests iron deficiency. What do you order to confirm it biochemically before starting treatment?

options.feedback_correct: Correct. Ferritin is the most sensitive marker for iron deficiency — it reflects total body iron stores. In iron deficiency: ferritin low, serum iron low, TIBC high. Together these make the diagnosis airtight.
options.pt_remark_correct: So the numbers will prove it. That something is actually wrong. That I wasn't just... weak.

options.feedback_wrong: Hypothyroidism causes fatigue and depression — but it causes macrocytic or normocytic anaemia, not microcytic. The CBC is already showing you the picture. Follow it.
options.pt_remark_wrong: My thyroid. Could it be my thyroid? I hadn't thought of that.

options.feedback_wrong: She is not depressed. Checking whether she is taking her sertraline misses the point entirely. The CBC is telling you what is wrong.
options.pt_remark_wrong: You want to check if I've been taking the pills. I have. Every morning. They just... don't work.

options.feedback_wrong: There is nothing in this presentation to suggest intracranial pathology. The CBC has already handed you the diagnosis. An MRI brain tells you nothing useful here.
options.pt_remark_wrong: There's something in my brain? I — I don't think it's my brain.

management.title: First-line treatment
management.hint: Iron deficiency confirmed. Hgb 7.4. She is symptomatic but haemodynamically stable. What do you start?

options.text: Oral ferrous sulfate 325mg TID with vitamin C
options.feedback_correct: Correct. Oral ferrous sulfate with vitamin C is first-line.
fennick_speech: "Stop. Before you write that prescription — look at her."
"The dry riverbed of her veins is about to receive rain for the first time in months. The iron will flow back. The red will return — slowly, not all at once, but it will return."

"In a week her reticulocytes will surge. In four weeks her Hgb will climb. And somewhere around week six... she will reach for a needle without thinking about it. Not because she decided to. Because her hands will remember before she does."
"We did not treat depression today. We returned the thread to the woman who makes beautiful things."
options.pt_remark_correct: Iron. Red. I used to know what that felt like.

options.feedback_wrong: IV iron is for patients who cannot tolerate oral iron or have malabsorption. Hgb 7.4 with no haemodynamic compromise — oral iron is appropriate. Do not over-escalate.
options.pt_remark_wrong: I can take anything. A drip, pills, whatever. I just want to feel like myself again.

options.feedback_wrong: Transfusion threshold in stable non-bleeding patients is Hgb below 7.0 — and even then, only with cardiovascular compromise. She is tachycardic but maintaining. Oral iron is correct.
options.pt_remark_wrong: A transfusion sounds frightening. Is it that bad?

options.feedback_wrong: There is no depression to treat. The sadness, the anhedonia, the fog — all manifestations of severe anaemia. Increasing her sertraline will do precisely nothing.
options.pt_remark_wrong: More sertraline. I knew you'd say more sertraline.

management.title: Address the underlying cause
management.hint: Treating the anaemia without addressing why it developed is incomplete medicine. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: Refer to gynaecology for heavy menstrual bleeding
options.feedback_correct: Correct. Heavy menstrual bleeding is the most common cause of iron deficiency anaemia in premenopausal women — and she has been losing blood every cycle for years without anyone asking about it. Gynaecology referral for workup. You are not just treating a number. You are treating a person who has been quietly bleeding out.
options.pt_remark_correct: Someone is finally going to ask about it. In six years, no one has ever asked about it.

options.feedback_wrong: Diet is a contributing factor but dietary changes alone cannot compensate for seven days of heavy menstrual bleeding every month. Address the source.
options.pt_remark_wrong: I'll eat more. I will. I know I haven't been.

options.text: Upper GI endoscopy to rule out bleeding ulcer
options.feedback_wrong: GI investigation is appropriate in postmenopausal women or men where the source is not obvious. In a 29-year-old woman with heavy periods, the source is almost certainly menstrual. Do not scope her before addressing the obvious cause.
options.pt_remark_wrong: A scope. Inside. I — alright. If you think it's necessary.

options.text: No further workup — iron stores will replete with supplements
options.feedback_wrong: Treating the anaemia without finding why it keeps recurring means she will be back in six months with the same Hgb. The underlying cause is gynaecological. Patching a leak without finding the pipe.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Alright. Just the iron then.

management.title: Disposition and follow-up
management.hint: Stable patient, new diagnosis, outpatient-appropriate condition. What is your plan?

options.text: Discharge — ferrous sulfate, stop sertraline, gynaecology referral, recheck CBC in 4 weeks
options.feedback_correct: Exactly right. She goes home with iron, a clear explanation of what was actually wrong with her, instructions to stop the sertraline, and a referral to gynaecology. Recheck CBC and iron studies in 4 weeks. Tell her the ice cravings will go away once her iron is repleted. Tell her she is not broken.
options.pt_remark_correct: Four weeks. I can hold on for four weeks. And then — the needles again. The red thread.

options.text: Admit for IV iron and monitoring
options.feedback_wrong: There is no indication for admission. She is haemodynamically stable, not actively bleeding, and oral iron will work.
options.pt_remark_wrong: I don't need to stay here. I just need to go home.

options.text: Discharge with increased sertraline dose and iron
options.feedback_wrong: The depression is the anaemia. Once the iron is repleted, the mood will follow — reliably, within weeks. Continuing the sertraline perpetuates a misdiagnosis.
options.pt_remark_wrong: More sertraline. I — I'll take it. If you think it's right.

options.text: Refer to psychiatry for treatment-resistant depression
options.feedback_wrong: Referring her to psychiatry now would be the final insult of a long diagnostic failure. She does not have treatment-resistant depression. She has a Hgb of 7.4 and empty iron stores.
options.pt_remark_wrong: I can see a psychiatrist. If that's what you think is wrong.

fennick_case_complete: "She came in here carrying a diagnosis someone handed her because they did not look hard enough. Depression. Eight weeks of sertraline for a woman whose body was simply running out of the thing that makes blood red."
"Every tear at her workbench. Every dropped stitch. Every morning she stood at the bottom of those stairs and wondered why her legs felt like iron — because they were starved of it."
"We did not cure her today. We found her. That is what diagnosis actually is — not naming a disease, but finding the person underneath it."
"She will pick up the red thread again. I promise you that."

report_template.pmh: previously misdiagnosed depression, dysmenorrhea, and poor nutritional intake
report_template.presenting: four months of progressive fatigue, low mood, and functional decline — referred by employer after errors at work

pertinent_positives.addl-hx: seven to eight day heavy menstrual cycles with clots, pagophagia (ice craving), dyspnea on exertion, no improvement on eight weeks of sertraline, skipping meals since November
pertinent_positives.pe: conjunctival pallor, koilonychia, angular cheilitis, glossitis, diffuse hair thinning, compensatory tachycardia rate 108 with soft systolic flow murmur
pertinent_positives.labs: Hgb 7.4 critically low, MCV 64 microcytic, RDW 18.2 elevated, reactive thrombocytosis — classic iron deficiency pattern
pertinent_positives.imaging: CXR without acute findings, functional flow murmur confirmed not structural
pertinent_positives.special: peripheral smear showing hypochromic microcytic RBCs with pencil cells and target cells consistent with iron deficiency anaemia

report_template.assessment: Iron Deficiency Anaemia secondary to chronic heavy menstrual blood loss and nutritional deficiency, presenting as treatment-resistant depression
report_template.plan: discontinue sertraline, start ferrous sulfate 325mg TID with vitamin C, gynaecology referral for heavy menstrual bleeding workup, dietary counselling, recheck CBC and iron studies in 4 weeks

explanation: Iron deficiency anaemia is the most common anaemia worldwide. In premenopausal women, heavy menstrual bleeding is the leading cause. The classic presentation is microcytic hypochromic anaemia — low Hgb, low MCV, low ferritin, high TIBC. Peripheral smear: pencil cells, hypochromic microcytic RBCs. Clinical signs: koilonychia, angular cheilitis, glossitis, pagophagia (pica for ice). Fatigue and mood disturbance are common and completely reversible with iron repletion. Treatment: oral ferrous sulfate with vitamin C. Address the underlying cause.

explanation_detail.who_gets_it: Premenopausal women (menstrual blood loss), pregnant women, infants, adolescents, anyone with chronic GI blood loss or poor dietary intake.
explanation_detail.risk_factors: Heavy menstrual bleeding, poor diet, malabsorption (celiac, IBD), chronic GI blood loss (ulcer, colorectal cancer in older patients), pregnancy.
explanation_detail.typical_presentation: Fatigue, pallor, dyspnea on exertion, palpitations. Signs: koilonychia (spoon nails), angular cheilitis, glossitis, pagophagia (pica for ice). Mood disturbance and cognitive fog are common and reversible.
explanation_detail.moa: Iron deficiency → impaired haemoglobin synthesis → microcytic hypochromic RBCs → reduced oxygen carrying capacity → compensatory tachycardia, high-output murmur. Ferritin depletes first (before Hgb falls).
explanation_detail.step1_buzzwords: Microcytic hypochromic anaemia, low ferritin, low serum iron, high TIBC, koilonychia, pagophagia, pencil cells, reactive thrombocytosis.
explanation_detail.lab_buzzwords: Low Hgb, low MCV (<80), low MCH, high RDW, low ferritin (most sensitive), low serum iron, high TIBC. Peripheral smear: pencil cells, target cells, hypochromic microcytic RBCs.
explanation_detail.treatment: Oral ferrous sulfate 325mg TID with vitamin C (enhances absorption). Take on empty stomach if tolerated. Reticulocytosis in 5-10 days, Hgb normalises in 4-8 weeks. Continue 3-6 months after normalisation to replete stores. Treat underlying cause.

title: The Pianist's Hands

chart.cc: Something is wrong with my hands. I thought it was age. I've been telling myself that for eight months.
chart.hpi: Maestro Voss arrived alone. He was the principal pianist of the Ferrovane Meridian Concert Hall for thirty-one years. He has been teaching at the Conservatory since his retirement four years ago. Over eight months, sensation in his fingertips has been fading. He noticed it first in his right index finger during a lesson — a student asked why he wasn't demonstrating the passage and he realized he could not feel which key he was pressing. He has told no one. The numbness has moved into his feet. He fell crossing the stage last Tuesday. His students saw. He is here because a colleague insisted.
chart.pmh: Mild dyspepsia for several years. Consulted a guild physician eight months ago — was given a diagnosis he does not fully understand and sent home.
chart.meds: None prescribed. Occasional antacid for dyspepsia.
chart.fh: Mother: thyroid disease. No known neurological family history.
chart.sh: Retired concert pianist. Teaches at the Ferrovane Conservatory, three mornings per week. Lives alone in a flat above the Meridian Concert Hall. Strict vegetarian for thirty-two years — ethical conviction. Does not drink. Does not smoke.

cc_after_addl_hx: She always sat in the third row, center. Every performance, thirty-one years. I still set a place at the table. I know it's foolish.
cc_after_pe: The tuning fork. I know what you're checking. I tried it on my piano last week. I pressed it to the case and felt nothing. I've felt that resonance every day for fifty years.
cc_after_imaging: The spine. I thought it was my hands. But it goes deeper than the hands, doesn't it.
cc_after_hint: Just tell me — is there time. Is there still time to fix this.
cc_after_dx: Pernicious. That is the word. All these months and it has a name. And it was... taking me apart. From the inside. Without telling me.
fennick_after_addl_hx: "Forty-seven years of music. And his body has been quietly dismantling the mechanism that lets him feel it. Not age. Look at what the stomach is telling you."
fennick_after_pe: "Upper and lower motor neuron signs in the same patient. Two different tracts. One cord. Think about what process does that — and think about what the blood is telling you simultaneously."
fennick_after_imaging: "Posterior and lateral. Both. Not a compressive lesion. Not demyelinating plaques. Symmetric, bilateral, continuous from cervical to thoracic. The distribution is the diagnosis if you know what you're looking at."
additional_hx: He answers carefully, the way someone does when they have been practicing composure. The numbness began in his right index finger eight months ago — he is certain of the date because he was mid-lesson when a student asked why he wasn't demonstrating the passage. He realized he could not feel which key he was pressing. He has told no one. The numbness now extends to all fingers bilaterally, to the mid-forearm, and in his feet to the ankle. He stumbles on uneven ground and holds the wall on stairs. He has had intermittent dyspepsia for several years — early satiety, mild bloating — never investigated because he assumed it was dietary. He has been a strict vegetarian for thirty-two years. His wife died four years ago. He stopped performing the week she was diagnosed. He has never said aloud that the music feels different now — that he can hear everything but feel almost nothing under his hands.
patient_quote_addl_hx: "There is a Bösendorfer in my flat. I play every morning. I can hear when it is right. But I cannot feel the difference between the keys anymore — not really. I know the fingering. I know the sound. But the... the conversation between my hands and the instrument. It has gone quiet."
pe_findings: General: Thin, quietly composed. Mild pallor. Faint icteric tinge to sclerae. Tongue smooth and pale at lateral edges. Neurological: Vibration perception: absent bilaterally at ankles, absent at wrists, absent to knee on the right. Position sense: markedly impaired at the toes bilaterally, impaired at fingers. Unable to maintain balance with eyes closed — falls to the right. Gait: wide-based, stamping quality, markedly worsened with eyes closed. Patellar reflexes: brisk, 3+ bilaterally. Ankle reflexes: absent bilaterally. Plantar responses: upgoing bilaterally. Upper limbs: reduced light touch in a distal-to-proximal gradient. Fine motor: cannot perform finger opposition sequence accurately with eyes closed.
imaging: MRI Spine (cervical and thoracic, T2): Bilateral symmetric increased T2 signal involving the posterior aspect and lateral aspect of the cord — present at cervical and upper thoracic levels, continuous. Axial cross-sections show a characteristic symmetric posterior signal pattern. No compressive lesion. No disc herniation. No cord atrophy. No enhancing lesion on post-contrast sequences.

special_test.name: Serum B12 + Anti-Intrinsic Factor Antibodies
special_test.result: Serum B12: 68 pmol/L (reference 150–700). Methylmalonic acid: markedly elevated. Homocysteine: markedly elevated. Anti-intrinsic factor antibodies: POSITIVE. Anti-parietal cell antibodies: POSITIVE. Serum gastrin: elevated.

memory_fragments.cbc: His handwriting was famous at the Conservatory — perfect notation, the stems exactly the right length, dynamics marked with absolute precision. His students kept his annotated scores. He has not written a note in four months.
memory_fragments.pe_vibration: He pressed a tuning fork to the case of the Bösendorfer last week. He has done this every morning for fifty years — felt the resonance travel through the wood into his palm, the way it used to travel from the keys up through the hammers into the strings. He felt nothing. He stood at the piano for a long time after that.
memory_fragments.mri: The posterior columns carry sensation upward. The lateral columns carry the command to move. Both are fraying — like strings losing their tension one by one, silently, in the dark of a closed piano.

tests.name: CBC
tests.result: Hgb 9.8 g/dL. MCV 114 fL. Hypersegmented neutrophils on peripheral smear. WBC 4.2. Platelets 138.
tests.memory_fragment: His handwriting was famous at the Conservatory — perfect notation, the stems exactly the right length, dynamics marked with absolute precision. His students kept his annotated scores. He has not written a note in four months.

tests.name: CMP
tests.result: LDH elevated (intravascular hemolysis from megaloblastic destruction). Indirect bilirubin mildly elevated. Creatinine 1.0. LFTs normal. No metabolic cause for neuropathy.

tests.name: EKG
tests.result: Normal sinus rhythm. No ischemic changes. Not directly contributory.

tests.name: Troponin
tests.result: Negative. No cardiac injury.

tests.name: Lipase
tests.result: Normal.

tests.name: UA
tests.result: Normal. No protein, no casts. No diabetic nephropathy pattern.

tests.name: ABG/VBG
tests.result: Normal. pO2 96, pH 7.41. Anaemia is compensated.

tests.name: Coags
tests.result: Normal. No coagulopathy.

hints: Upper motor neuron signs and lower motor neuron signs in the same patient. Hyperreflexia at the knees, absent ankle jerks, upgoing plantars. A central process affecting two different tracts simultaneously. The MRI tells you exactly where.
The MCV is 114. The smear has hypersegmented neutrophils. He is a strict vegetarian of 32 years with autoimmune markers in his bloodwork and elevated gastrin. These are not separate facts.

He has been treating dyspepsia with antacids for years. His gastrin is elevated, his parietal cell antibodies are positive, and his serum B12 is 68. The stomach is the beginning of this story, not the nervous system.

Folate deficiency produces the same blood picture. Treating it corrects the anaemia and makes everything look better — while the cord continues to deteriorate silently. The choice of what to treat first, and how to give it, is the whole case.

management.title: Confirm the diagnosis biochemically
management.hint: The MRI and clinical picture strongly suggest B12 deficiency. What confirms it — and distinguishes pernicious anemia from dietary deficiency?

options.text: Serum B12, methylmalonic acid, homocysteine, anti-intrinsic factor antibodies
options.feedback_correct: Correct. Serum B12 alone can be falsely normal in early or masked deficiency. Methylmalonic acid and homocysteine are the functional markers — both elevated when B12 is truly depleted at the cellular level. The antibody results will determine the mechanism and dictate the route of treatment.
options.pt_remark_correct: So there is a test. There is something in the blood that will say what is wrong. After eight months of nothing.

options.feedback_wrong: Thyroid disease can cause neuropathy but does not explain posterior column signs, macrocytic anaemia, or an inverted V on spinal MRI. You have a much more specific picture in front of you.
options.pt_remark_wrong: The thyroid. My mother had that. Is that — is that what this is?

options.feedback_wrong: NCS will confirm peripheral neuropathy but cannot diagnose the cause or detect the posterior column involvement. You already have the MRI. Follow the biochemistry.
options.pt_remark_wrong: More tests on the nerves. I've had those. They told me peripheral neuropathy. Just peripheral neuropathy.

options.text: HbA1c to rule out diabetic neuropathy
options.feedback_wrong: Diabetic neuropathy is peripheral only — it does not cause posterior column signs, Babinski, or MRI cord signal change. And he has macrocytic, not normocytic anaemia.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Diabetes. I've never had sugar problems. I've been careful my whole life.

management.title: First-line treatment
management.hint: B12 critically low, pernicious anemia confirmed, active cord demyelination. What is the correct route and why?

options.text: IM hydroxocobalamin — loading doses then lifelong maintenance
options.sub: Intramuscular B12 — bypasses absent intrinsic factor
options.feedback_correct: Correct. The antibody profile tells you the mechanism — parietal cell destruction, absent intrinsic factor. Oral supplementation will not be absorbed regardless of dose. The only route that works is intramuscular. Loading doses then lifelong maintenance.
fennick_speech: "Before you give the injection — understand what you are giving back."
"The central lesions — the cord signal you saw on imaging — some of that has been dark too long. That will not fully return."
"But the peripheral nerves regenerate. In six weeks his fingertips will begin to feel the keys again. Not perfectly. Enough."
"That is what this is. Not a cure. The return of sensation to a man who has been playing music he could no longer feel."
options.pt_remark_correct: A needle. Every week. I can do that. I have been waiting eight months for something to do.

options.feedback_wrong: He cannot absorb oral B12. The route matters as much as the drug. You have the antibody results. Act on them.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Pills. I've already tried changing my diet. I've been eating carefully my whole life.

options.feedback_wrong: This corrects the blood count. It does not address the cord. And once the anaemia improves the picture looks better — while below the surface, nothing has changed. This is the trap.
options.pt_remark_wrong: A vitamin. You think this is a vitamin deficiency?

options.feedback_wrong: Thiamine is for Wernicke encephalopathy — confusion, ophthalmoplegia, ataxia in an alcoholic or malnourished patient. He is none of those things and the MRI shows posterior and lateral column signal, not mammillary body involvement.
options.pt_remark_wrong: I don't drink. I've never drunk.

management.title: Investigate for associated conditions
management.hint: Pernicious anemia is an autoimmune condition. Autoimmune gastritis carries a specific long-term risk. What do you screen for?

options.sub: Screen for gastric atrophy and gastric cancer
options.feedback_correct: Correct. Autoimmune gastritis causes chronic atrophic gastritis which carries a 3–6x increased risk of gastric carcinoid tumours and adenocarcinoma. Upper endoscopy at diagnosis is standard. Also assess for H. pylori.
options.pt_remark_correct: A camera. Down the throat. I understand. Whatever it takes.

options.feedback_wrong: Lung cancer is not the associated risk here. The autoimmune gastritis that causes pernicious anemia increases gastric cancer risk specifically. The correct investigation is upper endoscopy.
options.pt_remark_wrong: The lungs. I don't smoke. I've never smoked.

options.text: No further workup — treat and discharge
options.feedback_wrong: Pernicious anemia is not just a B12 problem — it is a marker of autoimmune gastritis with ongoing malignancy risk. Sending him home without endoscopic surveillance is incomplete medicine.
options.pt_remark_wrong: So that's it then. Just the injection and I go.

options.feedback_wrong: Colonoscopy is standard cancer screening for his age but is not the specific concern here. The gastric risk from autoimmune gastritis takes priority.
options.pt_remark_wrong: The bowel. Is this about the bowel.

management.title: Disposition and prognosis
management.hint: Stable patient, diagnosis confirmed, treatment started. What does he need to know and where does he go?

options.text: Discharge — lifelong IM B12, neurology follow-up, physiotherapy, honest prognosis
options.feedback_correct: Correct. He is medically stable. He needs to understand: neurological recovery is partial and slow. The longest-demyelinated axons will not fully recover. But sensation in the fingertips — the peripheral nerves — will regenerate meaningfully over 6–12 weeks. Tell him the truth. He is a man who has been living with uncertainty for eight months. He can handle the truth.
options.pt_remark_correct: Six weeks. I'll count them. There is sheet music on the stand. I haven't touched it in four months.

options.feedback_wrong: IV B12 is not standard and confers no benefit over IM in a stable patient. He is haemodynamically stable and neurologically compensated. Outpatient IM loading is appropriate.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Admitted. To a ward. For how long.

options.text: Discharge with oral B12 and outpatient follow-up
options.feedback_wrong: He has pernicious anemia. Oral B12 will not be absorbed. You have already established this.
options.pt_remark_wrong: I'll take whatever you say. I just want to feel the keys again.

options.text: Refer to psychiatry for grief counselling
options.feedback_wrong: He is grieving, yes. And he deserves support for that separately. But his neurological decline is not grief. Referring him to psychiatry without completing medical management abandons him at the moment he finally has an answer.
options.pt_remark_wrong: A psychiatrist. You think this is in my head.

fennick_case_complete: "He came in here believing his hands had simply finished. Eight months of that. Eight months of performing in front of students and feeling nothing under his fingers and telling himself this was what ending felt like."
"It wasn't ending. It was a protein his stomach stopped making years ago — quietly, without symptoms he would have noticed — and the nervous system paid the debt one axon at a time."
"The posterior columns will not fully heal. Some of what was lost is gone. But the fingertips will come back. In six weeks he will press a key and feel it."
"He will go back to that hall. He will sit at the piano. And for the first time in four years, he will play for her and actually feel every note."
tier3_silhouette_description: An old man seated at a grand piano in an empty concert hall, back to us, hands resting on the keys. Not playing. Just resting. On top of the piano: a framed photograph, barely visible in the amber light.
polaroid_caption_success: He played the Goldberg Variations on a Thursday afternoon in April. The hall was empty. He said she was there. He was probably right.
polaroid_caption_failure: The piano remained closed. His students said he still came to teach. But he never played for them again.

report_template.pmh: peripheral neuropathy previously attributed to aging, chronic dyspepsia, strict vegetarianism for 32 years
report_template.presenting: eight months of progressive distal sensory loss, gait instability, and deterioration of fine motor function

pertinent_positives.addl-hx: right index finger numbness first — noticed during piano lesson, now bilateral glove-stocking neuropathy, falls on uneven ground, unable to feel key texture
pertinent_positives.pe: absent vibration and proprioception bilaterally, positive Romberg, hyperreflexia at knees, absent ankle jerks, bilateral Babinski — classic subacute combined degeneration pattern
pertinent_positives.labs: Hgb 9.8 macrocytic MCV 114 with hypersegmented neutrophils, elevated MMA and homocysteine, serum B12 critically low at 68 pmol/L
pertinent_positives.imaging: MRI spine with bilateral T2 hyperintensity in posterior and lateral columns — inverted V sign on axial imaging
pertinent_positives.special: anti-intrinsic factor antibodies positive and anti-parietal cell antibodies positive — confirming pernicious anemia as the aetiology

report_template.assessment: Vitamin B12 Deficiency secondary to Pernicious Anemia, complicated by subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord
report_template.plan: IM hydroxocobalamin loading then lifelong maintenance, upper endoscopy for gastric cancer surveillance, neurology follow-up, physiotherapy, counselling regarding partial neurological recovery

explanation: Vitamin B12 deficiency from pernicious anemia causes subacute combined degeneration — demyelination of the posterior columns (vibration, proprioception) and lateral columns (corticospinal tract), producing a mixed UMN/LMN picture. The inverted V on MRI is pathognomonic. Treatment requires IM B12 — oral B12 is useless without intrinsic factor. Folate alone is dangerous — it corrects the anaemia while cord demyelination continues silently.

explanation_detail.who_gets_it: Elderly patients, strict vegans/vegetarians, patients with autoimmune gastritis (pernicious anemia), postgastrectomy, metformin use (reduces B12 absorption).
explanation_detail.risk_factors: Pernicious anemia (most common in elderly), strict vegan diet without supplementation, gastric surgery, Crohn's disease affecting terminal ileum, metformin long-term.
explanation_detail.typical_presentation: Subacute combined degeneration: posterior column (vibration, proprioception loss, positive Romberg, ataxia) + lateral column (hyperreflexia, Babinski) + peripheral neuropathy (absent ankle jerks). Megaloblastic anaemia: high MCV, hypersegmented neutrophils. Neuropsychiatric: cognitive slowing, depression.
explanation_detail.moa: B12 essential for myelin synthesis and DNA replication. Deficiency → impaired methylation → demyelination of posterior and lateral spinal columns. Pernicious anemia: autoimmune destruction of parietal cells → absent intrinsic factor → B12 malabsorption regardless of dietary intake.
explanation_detail.step1_buzzwords: Subacute combined degeneration, inverted V sign MRI, macrocytic anaemia, hypersegmented neutrophils, anti-intrinsic factor antibodies, absent vibration + hyperreflexia + Babinski, MMA elevated.
explanation_detail.lab_buzzwords: Low B12, high MCV, high MMA (most sensitive), high homocysteine, positive anti-intrinsic factor antibodies (specific for pernicious anemia), hypersegmented neutrophils on smear.
explanation_detail.treatment: IM hydroxocobalamin — bypass absent intrinsic factor. NEVER oral in pernicious anemia. NEVER folate alone (masks anaemia, cord continues to demyelinate). Loading: daily IM x1 week, then every 3 months lifelong. Neurological recovery: partial — peripheral nerves regenerate, central lesions may be permanent.

title: The Morning Piece
polaroid_caption_success: On a Tuesday morning in May, he played the whole piece without stopping. His student said it was correct. Every note. She did not tell him that. She just listened.
polaroid_caption_failure: He played every morning until he didn't. The piece was always the same. No one ever learned its name.

chart.cc: My student brought me. I told her I was fine. I am not sure what fine means anymore.
chart.hpi: Maestro Driscoll was brought in by his student of eleven years, Mira, who did not call ahead because she was not sure he would still be there when she returned. His wife Elise died seven months ago. He has been alone in their flat since. Mira visits three times a week for lessons that have become something else — she arrives and finds him at the piano, playing. Always the same piece. He finishes it, then looks up and sometimes knows her, and sometimes does not. On good days he is entirely himself — sharp, precise, withering about intonation. On bad days he asks her where Elise is. He has fallen twice in the past month. He has begun sleeping in the chair because he says the bed is wrong. Mira found him on the floor on Thursday morning. He told her he had been performing.
chart.pmh: Hypertension — well controlled. No prior neurological history. No diabetes.
chart.fh: Father: died of 'memory problems' in his eighties, details unknown.
chart.sh: Retired concert pianist. Widowed seven months ago. Lives alone. Does not drink. Does not smoke. Plays piano daily — has done so for sixty years.

cc_after_addl_hx: I always play in the morning. I have always played in the morning. I don't remember choosing that piece. I just play it.
cc_after_pe: There was a woman in the audience last Tuesday. Third seat from the aisle. I played better. Mira said no one was there. Mira is wrong.
cc_after_imaging: Is it the brain. I always knew something was dimming. Like a candle in a room that is slowly getting larger.
cc_after_hint: Why do I play that piece every morning. I have been asking myself that for months. I thought I would remember eventually.
cc_after_dx: I see. Does that mean — will I forget her completely. Before the end. Will I forget what she looked like.
fennick_after_addl_hx: "Good days and bad days. Not a steady slope. That pattern means something specific — and it is not what most people will assume it is. And the piece he plays without knowing why. Do not move past that."
fennick_after_pe: "A woman in the third seat. He played better. The student saw an empty chair. You now have three things: the fluctuation, the movement, and that. Taken together they point somewhere very specific."
fennick_after_imaging: "The posterior cortex. The DaTscan. Between the two — and what you heard in that room — the answer is here. Synthesize it. Do not reach for the nearest diagnosis."
additional_hx: Mira speaks for long stretches while Maestro Driscoll listens with the focused patience of a man accustomed to waiting for others to finish. When she stops, he corrects one detail: he did not fall on Thursday. He was performing. He makes this distinction seriously. He describes days that are entirely clear — last Monday he corrected Mira's fingering on the Scarlatti with complete precision — and days where the flat feels unfamiliar and the faces of visitors take a moment to resolve. He mentions Elise only once: 'She would have wanted me to keep playing.' He says this without sadness, as a fact. He has been sleeping in the armchair for six weeks. The bed feels dangerous, he says, though he cannot explain why. He woke with a bruised wrist two weeks ago and does not know how it happened. He mentions, as an afterthought, that sometimes when he plays he can see people watching. He considers this a good sign.
patient_quote_addl_hx: "The piece I play — I learned it fifty years ago. I played it the night Elise first heard me perform. I did not know she was in the hall. She was in the third row, center. I found that out later. I don't know why I remember that now. I haven't thought about that in years."
pe_findings: General: Thin, upright, deliberate in movement. Mild masked facies. Reduced blink rate. Neurological: Cogwheel rigidity bilateral upper extremities, mild — asymmetric, right greater than left. Bradykinesia — finger tapping slowed. Gait: slightly stooped, reduced arm swing, short steps, no festination. No resting tremor. Postural instability — pull test mildly positive. Cognition: MMSE 21/30 — loses points on orientation, recall, and a drawing task. Marked improvement noted mid-examination when he discusses music — full sentences, precise vocabulary, rapid retrieval. Then asks if Mira has arrived yet. She is seated beside him. Visuospatial: clock drawing severely impaired — posterior cortical pattern. No focal weakness. No sensory deficit.
imaging: MRI Brain: Cortical atrophy, posterior predominant — parietal and occipital regions disproportionately affected. Mild hippocampal atrophy, less severe than expected for degree of cognitive impairment. No acute infarct. No mass lesion. No normal pressure hydrocephalus pattern. DaTscan (Dopamine Transporter Scan): Reduced dopamine transporter uptake in bilateral putamen and caudate — asymmetric, right greater than left. Markedly abnormal pattern.

special_test.name: Polysomnography (Sleep Study)
special_test.result: REM sleep without atonia confirmed. During REM sleep: complex vocalizations, limb movements, brief episode of sitting up with arms extended — posture consistent with performing. No seizure activity. No apnoea. Findings confirm REM sleep behavior disorder.

memory_fragments.datscan: There is a photograph on top of the piano. A woman in a concert hall, third row center, slightly blurred — taken from the stage. He does not know Mira took it. He does not know it is there. He plays toward it every morning.
memory_fragments.rbdsleep: He dreamed he was performing. The hall was full. She was in the third row. He played the final chord and his arm came down. He woke on the floor. He told Mira he had been performing. He was not wrong.
memory_fragments.hallucination: He sees her during the difficult passages. Not always. But when the music reaches the part that frightened him in 1978 — the descending run in the left hand — she appears in the third seat, watching. He plays it better when she is there. He has always played it better when she is there.

tests.name: CBC
tests.result: Normal. WBC 6.8, Hgb 13.4, Plt 221. No inflammatory process.

tests.name: CMP
tests.result: Normal. Creatinine 1.0, glucose 96, LFTs normal, TSH 1.8. No metabolic cause for cognitive change.

tests.name: EKG
tests.result: Normal sinus rhythm. No arrhythmia.

tests.name: Troponin
tests.result: Negative.

tests.name: Lipase
tests.result: Normal.

tests.name: UA
tests.result: Normal. No infection.

tests.name: ABG/VBG
tests.result: Normal.

tests.name: Coags
tests.result: Normal.

hints: The cognitive fluctuation — sharp on Monday, asking where his wife is on Wednesday. The motor findings without a resting tremor. The posterior cortical pattern on imaging. These are not three separate problems.

He sees a woman in the third seat. He plays better when she is there. The student sees an empty chair. This symptom has a specific character — formed, detailed, non-frightening. That character is diagnostically meaningful.
The DaTscan shows dopaminergic deficit. The posterior cortical atrophy on MRI. The RBD. The fluctuating cognition. One diagnosis produces all four simultaneously.

Before you choose a treatment — consider what class of drug will be given to manage his behavioral symptoms on the ward. Some medications that are standard elsewhere are not standard here. The choice could kill him.

management.title: Confirm the diagnosis
management.hint: You have a clinical picture and two imaging results. What confirms and characterizes the diagnosis before committing to treatment?

options.sub: Characterize cognitive profile and confirm RBD
options.feedback_correct: Correct. Neuropsychological testing will confirm the posterior cortical pattern and quantify the fluctuation. Polysomnography confirms REM sleep behavior disorder — which is not just clinically relevant but a core diagnostic criterion. The diagnosis is clinical; these tests characterize it and change what you tell the patient.
options.pt_remark_correct: More tests. I don't mind tests. I mind not knowing.

options.text: Lumbar puncture for CSF tau and amyloid-beta
options.feedback_wrong: Alzheimer biomarkers may be mildly abnormal but are not diagnostic here and do not change management. The clinical picture, DaTscan, and posterior imaging point away from typical Alzheimer's. The fluctuation and motor findings demand a different framework.
options.pt_remark_wrong: A needle in the spine. If that is what it takes.

options.feedback_wrong: The fluctuating cognition and witnessed nocturnal events could superficially suggest epilepsy, but the full picture — parkinsonism, posterior atrophy, DaTscan deficit, visual phenomena — is not a seizure syndrome. EEG is not the priority.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Seizures. I hadn't thought of that.

options.feedback_wrong: Treating before confirming is imprecise medicine. The choice of cholinesterase inhibitor matters — not all are equivalent here — and the diagnosis changes what you counsel the patient and family about prognosis and drug avoidance.
options.pt_remark_wrong: A medication. Already. Before we know what this is.

management.title: First-line pharmacotherapy
management.hint: Diagnosis confirmed. Cognitive fluctuation is the primary symptom affecting his function. What do you start — and what do you absolutely avoid?

options.text: Rivastigmine — cholinesterase inhibitor, start low and titrate
options.feedback_correct: Correct. Rivastigmine has the strongest evidence for this diagnosis specifically — stronger than for other dementia types. Start at 1.5mg BID with food, titrate slowly. It will not reverse the disease but meaningfully improves cognition and behavioral symptoms.
options.pt_remark_correct: A patch. I can do a patch. Mira will make sure I use it.

reactive_stage.fennick_transition: "Three days. The cognition has shifted — he remembered Mira's name yesterday without being told. But listen to what the night staff are reporting. We have solved one problem and uncovered another."
reactive_stage.pt_remark_transition: I played something yesterday. I remembered the fingering for the third movement. I haven't remembered that in months.
reactive_stage.vitals_note: Day 3 post-rivastigmine. Cognition marginally improved — oriented to person and place this morning. Night report: found beside bed on floor twice this week. Witnessed by night staff: sitting upright in bed, arms extended, vocalizing during sleep. Bruising on right forearm.
reactive_stage.title: Night staff report — new development
reactive_stage.hint: The treatment is working on one axis. Something else has surfaced that you already had evidence for — but now it is causing injury. Address it directly.

options.text: Low-dose clonazepam or melatonin at night
options.feedback_correct: Correct. The nocturnal episodes are REM sleep behavior disorder — he is acting out his dreams without the normal muscle atonia. He is performing in his sleep. Low-dose clonazepam (0.25–0.5mg) or high-dose melatonin are first-line. Protect him from injury — bed rail, floor mattress, clear the space beside the bed.
options.pt_remark_correct: I was performing. I was in the hall. She was in the third row. I was playing the right notes.

options.feedback_wrong: This is one of the most dangerous errors you can make in this diagnosis. Typical antipsychotics — and many atypical ones — can precipitate a severe, potentially fatal neuroleptic sensitivity reaction: acute parkinsonism, impaired consciousness, autonomic instability. The sensitivity rate exceeds 50%. Never give haloperidol here. Never.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Something to make me sleep. Yes. I would like to sleep properly.

options.feedback_wrong: The rivastigmine is working — escalating it does not address the nocturnal injury risk. These are separate mechanisms. Treat the RBD directly.
options.pt_remark_wrong: More of the same. Alright.

options.feedback_wrong: Zolpidem worsens confusion and falls in elderly patients and does not suppress the REM motor activity causing injury. It sedates without treating the mechanism.
options.pt_remark_wrong: A sleeping pill. I've never taken those.

options.feedback_wrong: Memantine has a role in moderate-to-severe stages but lacks the evidence of cholinesterase inhibitors as first-line here. This diagnosis specifically responds to the cholinergic pathway — that is where the deficit is most actionable.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Another medication. I hope it doesn't make my hands slow.

options.feedback_wrong: Levodopa can cautiously address motor symptoms but addressing cognitive decline — which is the presenting problem and what is destroying his ability to function — comes first. Levodopa also risks worsening hallucinations, which are already present.
options.pt_remark_wrong: The shaking. Yes. The shaking has been bothering me.

options.feedback_wrong: Haloperidol can precipitate a severe, potentially fatal neuroleptic sensitivity reaction in this diagnosis — acute parkinsonism, impaired consciousness, autonomic instability. Typical antipsychotics are contraindicated. This would be catastrophic.
options.pt_remark_wrong: Something to calm the difficult days. Yes. Those days are very difficult.

management.title: Disposition and safeguarding
management.hint: He is medically stabilized. He lives alone. What does going home safely require?

options.text: Discharge with carer support, medication alert card, specialist follow-up, no typical antipsychotics in notes
options.feedback_correct: Correct. He can go home — but not alone, not without structure. Community care package, Mira formally involved, a medication alert card documenting neuroleptic sensitivity. Any future admission or emergency must know: no haloperidol, no typical antipsychotics, no many atypical ones. This alert could save his life in a future emergency.
options.pt_remark_correct: I want to go home. The piano is there. I need to finish the piece.

options.text: Admit to memory care ward indefinitely
options.feedback_wrong: He is medically stable, the acute situation is addressed, and admission to an unfamiliar environment — with a diagnosis that causes fluctuating cognition and visual phenomena — risks significant decompensation. The familiar environment of his flat is protective, not hazardous, with appropriate support.
options.pt_remark_wrong: A ward. I see. Will there be a piano.

options.text: Discharge with olanzapine PRN for behavioral episodes
options.feedback_wrong: Olanzapine carries significant neuroleptic sensitivity risk in this diagnosis. PRN antipsychotics in the community without specialist supervision is dangerous. This is not appropriate.
options.pt_remark_wrong: A pill for the bad days. That sounds manageable.

options.text: Discharge without follow-up — stable on current medications
options.feedback_wrong: This is a progressive neurodegenerative condition with a specific and serious medication risk profile. Discharging without specialist follow-up, without a care plan, without a medication alert — and without telling Mira what to watch for — is abandonment dressed as efficiency.
options.pt_remark_wrong: I can manage on my own. I have always managed on my own.

fennick_case_complete: "He came in here playing a piece he did not know he had chosen. Every morning for seven months. The same piece. His hands knew something his conscious mind had not caught up to yet."
"The disease is taking him — the fluctuation will worsen, the motor symptoms will progress, the good days will become less reliable. We have bought him time. Not a cure. Time."
"But in that time — with the rivastigmine, with the nights now quiet — he will have mornings where he is entirely himself. Precise. Withering about intonation. Hers."
"He will play that piece again. And at some point, on some morning, he will remember why he plays it. That moment is not ours. It was always going to be his."

report_template.pmh: hypertension well-controlled, widowed seven months ago
report_template.presenting: progressive cognitive fluctuation, parkinsonism, nocturnal behavioral episodes, and recurrent falls — brought in by his student after witnessed floor fall

pertinent_positives.addl-hx: cognitive fluctuation with sharp good days and disoriented bad days, visual phenomena of a formed non-threatening nature, REM behavioral episodes with injury, piano playing preserved as isolated procedural memory
pertinent_positives.pe: cogwheel rigidity asymmetric right greater than left, bradykinesia, posterior cortical clock drawing impairment, MMSE 21/30, marked intratest fluctuation
pertinent_positives.labs: normal metabolic panel, TSH normal — no reversible cause
pertinent_positives.imaging: MRI posterior predominant cortical atrophy, DaTscan reduced dopamine transporter uptake bilateral putamen asymmetric
pertinent_positives.special: polysomnography confirming REM sleep without atonia with complex motor behaviors

report_template.assessment: Lewy Body Dementia — probable, meeting core and supportive criteria
report_template.plan: rivastigmine titration, clonazepam for RBD, medication alert card documenting neuroleptic sensitivity, community care package, neurology/specialist memory service follow-up, carer education

explanation: Lewy Body Dementia is characterized by four core features: cognitive fluctuation, visual hallucinations (formed, detailed, non-threatening), parkinsonism, and REM sleep behavior disorder. DaTscan shows dopaminergic deficit. MRI shows posterior cortical predominance. Cholinesterase inhibitors (rivastigmine) are first-line — they have stronger evidence here than in Alzheimer's. Neuroleptic sensitivity is the critical management risk: typical antipsychotics and many atypicals can cause severe, potentially fatal reactions. This is the fact that must follow this patient everywhere.

explanation_detail.who_gets_it: Adults over 60. Second most common cause of degenerative dementia after Alzheimer's. Frequently misdiagnosed as Alzheimer's or Parkinson's Disease Dementia.
explanation_detail.risk_factors: Age, male sex, family history. REM sleep behavior disorder can precede dementia by decades — it is a prodromal marker.
explanation_detail.typical_presentation: Fluctuating cognition (not steady decline), recurrent formed visual hallucinations, parkinsonism (bradykinesia, rigidity — resting tremor less prominent than PD), REM sleep behavior disorder. Posterior cortical atrophy on MRI. DaTscan abnormal.
explanation_detail.moa: Abnormal alpha-synuclein aggregates (Lewy bodies) in cortical and subcortical neurons — cholinergic and dopaminergic systems primarily affected. Differs from PD dementia by temporal relationship: dementia precedes or occurs simultaneously with parkinsonism.
explanation_detail.step1_buzzwords: Fluctuating cognition, visual hallucinations (formed, detailed), parkinsonism, RBD, DaTscan positive, posterior cortical atrophy, neuroleptic sensitivity, rivastigmine.
explanation_detail.lab_buzzwords: No specific blood marker. DaTscan: reduced uptake in putamen and caudate. PSG: REM without atonia. MRI: posterior > hippocampal atrophy (opposite of Alzheimer's).
explanation_detail.treatment: Rivastigmine (strongest evidence of all dementias). Clonazepam or melatonin for RBD. Levodopa cautiously for motor symptoms. ABSOLUTE CONTRAINDICATION: typical antipsychotics, many atypicals (risperidone, olanzapine — high sensitivity risk). Quetiapine or clozapine if antipsychotic unavoidable.
